news_summary: Innovative efforts are advancing to preserve climate science, implement ambitious tree planting, promote equitable environmental responsibility, and design climate-smart apartments to combat heat.
news_summary_fr: Des initiatives innovantes sont mises en œuvre pour préserver la science du climat, mener à bien des programmes ambitieux de plantation d'arbres, promouvoir une responsabilité environnementale équitable et concevoir des appartements adaptés au changement climatique afin de lutter contre la chaleur.
news_summary_es: Se están llevando a cabo iniciativas innovadoras para preservar la ciencia climática, poner en marcha ambiciosos programas de plantación de árboles, promover una responsabilidad medioambiental equitativa y diseñar apartamentos adaptados al clima para combatir el calor.

articles.title: The plan to make climate science harder to erase
articles.content:
When Rebecca Lindsey was fired from the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration last February, the first thing she did was stew. Then she worried about what was going to happen to the website she and her team had built over the last decade and a half. Lindsey had long been the lead writer and editor, and more recently the program manager, of Climate.gov, a site that distilled the agency’s research on climate change into easy-to-understand, free resources for the public.

She was right to be concerned: Within a matter of months, the Trump administration had eliminated the rest of the staff supporting Climate.gov and shut down the website — ironically, to comply with an executive order calling for “restoring gold standard science.”

“I couldn’t stand the thought of it all being thrown away,” Lindsey said of the website, which had been used by teachers, community leaders, and policymakers. It had also given researchers in the government important insight into what everyday Americans needed to know about climate science and how to answer their questions effectively. Members of the former Climate.gov team met periodically to discuss what could be done to preserve the work. By the end of last summer, they’d decided to create an independent version of the site. It launched late last month with a new nongovernmental domain: Climate.us.

The intent behind Climate.us isn’t just to save what was on the Climate.gov website when it died, but to continue to update it with new visuals, explainers, features, and Q&As, making climate science relevant to people with resources that are vetted by scientists. “We just try to constantly take the pulse of what scientists say is valuable and important and needs to be talked about and explained,” Lindsey said.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

Since its launch two weeks ago, the new site has gotten about 800,000 page views — an impressive number, considering that the old NOAA site had been getting about a million views a month, according to Lindsey.

After President Donald Trump took office a second time, some of the most easy-to-understand resources to help people understand the warming planet disappeared. The National Climate Assessments, congressionally mandated reports released every four years that translated the science into warnings for policymakers and the public, vanished last summer. In December, the Environmental Protection Agency removed at least 80 webpages about the causes, indicators, and effects of climate change. The EPA webpage explaining the causes of climate change no longer lists human activity as a direct driver of global warming. It now emphasizes — misleadingly — natural processes.

Izzy Pacenza, who monitors government websites for the Environmental Data and Governance Initiative, called it “an all-out assault on climate information.”

Thousands gather at the Lincoln Memorial in Washington, D.C., to defend science as a public good and central pillar of social progress in March 2025. Astrid Riecken / The Washington Post via Getty Images

Beyond the federal government

As organizations race to fill the gap left by the United States’ attack on its own scientific knowledge, many experts see an opportunity to shield research and data from the shifting winds of politics. The world’s science has relied on massive support from the U.S. government, but experts see a future that disperses some of its responsibilities, including how data is collected, handled, preserved, and used.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

“It can’t just be the federal government anymore,” said Janice Lachance, executive director and CEO of the American Geophysical Union, the largest Earth and space organization in the world. “That’s proven to us that that’s unreliable, that there’s too much control in very few hands. And so how do we distribute this to like-minded organizations, civil society, and [nongovernmental organizations] who care about it?

The American Geophysical Union is trying to fill the void where it can. It has launched a global initiative to ensure that environmental datasets are more resilient against threats such as political interference, pulling together a group of about 100 experts around the world. It’s also working with the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, the world’s leading authority on climate science, hosting an academic network that allows U.S. scientists to participate in key international reports even after the Trump administration withdrew from the group. Along with the American Meteorological Society, it has also released an invitation for climate manuscripts to maintain the research momentum of what would have been the sixth National Climate Assessment, with plans to eventually publish a special climate collection across different peer-reviewed journals.

For many former federal researchers like Lindsey, trying to carry on their previous work at nonprofits and through independent initiatives has been challenging.

Adam Smith, who led a project tracking billion-dollar weather and climate disasters at NOAA before the agency ended the program last year, has taken the work over to the nonprofit Climate Central. The project is now up and running with all the same data and methods, but it took almost a year to get it fully where it was back at NOAA. The research is important, Smith said, because it quantifies the economic effects of extreme weather, helping to communicate the real-world consequences of climate change to businesses, policymakers, and the public. He is working to develop the project further, documenting disasters that cost $100 million or more back to 1980.

Creating an independent copy of the Climate.gov site wasn’t easy, either. Researchers who had no experience fundraising had to crowdsource money and court philanthropists to back their work, Lindsey said. Web developers had to update all the old links that directed people to the defunct original site. The Climate.us team wanted independent scientific review for their materials, as they had done at NOAA, but some scientists declined to put their names on a defunded federal project because of unwanted publicity or fear of retaliation.

Lindsey managed to revive the site as one of just three full-time staff, compared to roughly eight people who were running the operation under NOAA full-time.

“In a lot of ways, I feel I’m back in 2010 when we first started building Climate.gov,” she said. “There are days when I think, ‘What am I doing? Do I have it in me to start this all over again?’”

These efforts to save climate information are crucial, experts said, but it’s tough for a patchwork of nonprofits, universities, and independent initiatives to fill the vacuum left by the federal government removing the most accessible resources about climate change. “No nonprofit is going to have the reach of the federal government, and so I think that there’s a massive gap in terms of people learning about where they can find these resources,” said Gretchen Gehrke, an environmental and public information researcher who co-founded the Environmental Data and Governance Initiative. Philanthropic funders can be fickle, too, raising questions about financial sustainability. “Truly, all of us are scrambling for funding and underfunded,” she said.

Nonprofits also don’t have the instant recognition that the government does, which can make it harder to earn public trust. When Smith started running the billion-dollar disaster project at Climate Central, for example, he found that some people didn’t know that anyone from NOAA was still involved. Now, the top of the website makes it clear that Climate Central is continuing NOAA’s dataset, with the same methods and the same lead scientist.

A sign that reads “NOAA Saves Lives” is seen in a corridor of the University of Colorado at Boulder in May. Ulysse Bellier / AFP via Getty Images

From rescue to reform

For information and data advocates, the current crisis is a wake-up call. “Guess what? We have really terrible and really insufficient data policies,” Gehrke said. As the Trump administration tests those vulnerabilities, it gives these stakeholders insight into what needs to change to protect government information from the political whims of future administrations. That could include writing specific requirements for agencies into law and building up Congress’ oversight capacity and enforcement mechanisms.

When public-facing platforms like Climate.gov disappear, people tend to wonder, How can we bring this product back? without examining the structural failures that led it to be vulnerable in the first place. Sonia Wang, senior director at the Data Foundation’s Center for Climate and Environmental Data, uses the metaphor that people usually focus on the fountain — the shiny map or platform — rather than the plumbing behind it. This invisible infrastructure is much more fragile than people realize, Wang said, sometimes relying on one person who’s been maintaining a dataset for decades, or relationships the federal government has built over time.

“This was always a problem, regardless of administration,” Wang said. “I think we’re just seeing more of the cracks be exposed now with the rapid decline in some of our federal partners being able to actually carry on their work without the staff.”

As organizations work to shore up the plumbing of the data that helps us understand the world, there’s increasingly a sense that they can’t count on government support like they did in the past. “It happened in the United States last year, and it continues this year, but it could happen anywhere,” Lachance said. “And we just don’t think that critical scientific data should be vulnerable to the political winds of the day.”
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant and inspiring effort by former federal scientists and nonprofit organizations to preserve and continue providing accessible, scientifically vetted climate change information despite political interference and government shutdowns. This initiative has broad societal impact by maintaining public access to crucial climate science resources, supporting informed decision-making, and promoting resilience of environmental data against political threats. The story is focused on a single topic with detailed context about the challenges, solutions, and ongoing efforts, demonstrating a meaningful positive real-world impact.
articles.personality_title: Scientists and nonprofits unite to keep climate science alive online
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In recent years, the U.S. government cut staff and shut down important websites that shared easy-to-understand information about climate change. This made it harder for people, teachers, and leaders to learn about climate science.

**What happened** – After the government closed Climate.gov, a popular website run by the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA), former staff created a new independent site called Climate.us. They worked hard to save the original content and continue updating it with new facts and answers, making sure the information is checked by scientists. This new website launched recently and has already attracted hundreds of thousands of visits.

**Impact** – This effort keeps reliable climate information available to the public despite political efforts to remove it. It helps people understand climate change better and supports important decisions. Also, organizations worldwide are working together to protect climate data from political changes, making science stronger and more trustworthy.

**What's next step** – These independent projects will keep growing, but they need funding and public trust to succeed. Experts say laws and stronger government oversight could help protect climate data in the future. Meanwhile, nonprofits and scientists continue to build new tools and reports to share climate science openly.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Former government scientists and nonprofits have created a new website to keep trusted climate science available to everyone, showing how teamwork can protect important information even when politics try to erase it.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques et des associations s’unissent pour préserver la science du climat en ligne
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Ces dernières années, le gouvernement américain a réduit le personnel et fermé des sites importants qui partageaient des informations faciles à comprendre sur le changement climatique. Cela a rendu plus difficile l’accès à ces informations pour le public, les enseignants et les dirigeants.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Après la fermeture de Climate.gov, un site populaire géré par la NOAA, les anciens membres de l’équipe ont créé un nouveau site indépendant appelé Climate.us. Ils ont travaillé dur pour sauver le contenu original et continuer à le mettre à jour avec de nouvelles informations vérifiées par des scientifiques. Ce nouveau site a été lancé récemment et a déjà attiré des centaines de milliers de visiteurs.

**Impact** – Cet effort maintient l’accès à une information fiable sur le climat malgré les tentatives politiques de la supprimer. Cela aide les gens à mieux comprendre le changement climatique et soutient des décisions importantes. De plus, des organisations du monde entier collaborent pour protéger les données climatiques des changements politiques, renforçant ainsi la science.

**Prochaine étape** – Ces projets indépendants vont continuer de grandir, mais ils ont besoin de financements et de la confiance du public pour réussir. Des experts estiment que des lois et un meilleur contrôle gouvernemental pourraient protéger ces données à l’avenir. En attendant, des associations et scientifiques développent de nouveaux outils et rapports pour partager la science du climat.

**Message clé en une phrase** – D’anciens scientifiques gouvernementaux et des associations ont créé un nouveau site pour garder la science fiable du climat accessible à tous, montrant que la collaboration peut protéger l’information importante même face à la politique.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos y organizaciones se unen para mantener viva la ciencia del clima en línea
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En los últimos años, el gobierno de Estados Unidos redujo personal y cerró sitios web importantes que compartían información fácil de entender sobre el cambio climático. Esto dificultó que el público, profesores y líderes accedieran a esta información.

**Qué pasó** – Después de que el gobierno cerró Climate.gov, un sitio popular dirigido por la NOAA, exmiembros del equipo crearon un nuevo sitio independiente llamado Climate.us. Trabajaron para salvar el contenido original y seguir actualizándolo con nuevos datos revisados por científicos. Este nuevo sitio se lanzó recientemente y ya ha recibido cientos de miles de visitas.

**Impacto** – Este esfuerzo mantiene disponible información confiable sobre el clima a pesar de los intentos políticos de eliminarla. Ayuda a las personas a entender mejor el cambio climático y apoya decisiones importantes. Además, organizaciones en todo el mundo colaboran para proteger los datos climáticos de los cambios políticos, fortaleciendo la ciencia.

**Próximo paso** – Estos proyectos independientes seguirán creciendo, pero necesitan financiamiento y la confianza del público para tener éxito. Expertos dicen que leyes y mayor supervisión gubernamental podrían proteger estos datos en el futuro. Mientras tanto, organizaciones y científicos continúan creando nuevas herramientas e informes para compartir la ciencia del clima.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Científicos exgubernamentales y organizaciones han creado un nuevo sitio para mantener la ciencia climática confiable accesible para todos, demostrando cómo el trabajo en equipo puede proteger información importante aunque la política intente borrarla.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a resilient lighthouse standing firm on a rocky shore, its light beam illuminating a rebuilt, glowing digital map composed of interconnected glowing data nodes and charts, symbolizing the preservation and continuation of climate science knowledge beyond political storms, rendered in soft natural earth tones and gentle blues.

articles.title: This 'ambitious' tree planting action plan is a first for NI
articles.summary: Northern Ireland has a target of achieving 12% woodland cover by 2050.
articles.content:
The current rate of tree planting in Northern Ireland will have to more than triple by 2032, to get on track for 12% woodland cover by 2050.

The first tree planting action plan for Northern Ireland, external sets out 10 strategic actions over the next five years.

The Environment Minister Andrew Muir said there was "lots to do", including raising awareness of the benefits of tree cover.

With just 8.6% woodland cover, Northern Ireland is one of the least wooded areas in Europe.

In UK terms, it lags behind Scotland (19%), Wales (15%), England (10%) and also the Republic of Ireland, which has 11% cover.

The new plan has been designed by the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs along with the Woodland Trust, representatives of Confederation of Forest Industries (CONFOR), forestry companies, eNGOs, Ulster Farmers' Union, NI Water, and local councils.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a concrete, strategic plan to significantly increase tree planting in Northern Ireland, aiming to boost woodland cover from 8.6% to 12% by 2050. This initiative involves multiple stakeholders and sets measurable targets, representing meaningful progress in conservation and climate action with broad environmental benefits.
articles.personality_title: Northern Ireland launches first plan to triple tree planting by 2032
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Northern Ireland currently has only 8.6% of its land covered by trees, making it one of the least wooded places in Europe. Other parts of the UK and Ireland have much higher tree coverage, like Scotland with 19% and the Republic of Ireland with 11%.

**What happened** – The Northern Ireland government has created its first-ever tree planting action plan. This plan aims to more than triple the current rate of tree planting by 2032. The goal is to increase woodland cover to 12% by the year 2050. The plan includes 10 key actions to be completed over the next five years. It was developed by the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs together with groups like the Woodland Trust, forestry companies, farmers, and local councils.

**Impact** – This plan is important because it sets clear goals and involves many different groups working together. Increasing tree cover will help the environment by improving air quality, supporting wildlife, and helping fight climate change. It also shows that Northern Ireland is taking a clear step toward protecting and improving its natural spaces.

**What's next step** – Over the next five years, the plan’s actions will be put into practice. This includes planting many more trees and raising public awareness about why trees matter. The government and partners will track progress to make sure the planting rate grows fast enough to meet the 2032 target.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Northern Ireland’s new tree planting plan aims to triple planting by 2032 to raise woodland cover from 8.6% to 12% by 2050, marking a major step for the region’s environment.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’Irlande du Nord lance son premier plan pour tripler la plantation d’arbres d’ici 2032
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’Irlande du Nord compte actuellement seulement 8,6 % de sa surface couverte par des arbres, ce qui en fait l’une des régions les moins boisées d’Europe. D’autres parties du Royaume-Uni et d’Irlande ont un couvert forestier bien plus élevé, comme l’Écosse avec 19 % et la République d’Irlande avec 11 %.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le gouvernement d’Irlande du Nord a créé son tout premier plan d’action pour la plantation d’arbres. Ce plan vise à plus que tripler le rythme actuel de plantation d’arbres d’ici 2032. L’objectif est d’augmenter la couverture forestière à 12 % d’ici 2050. Le plan comprend 10 actions clés à réaliser au cours des cinq prochaines années. Il a été élaboré par le Département de l’Agriculture, de l’Environnement et des Affaires rurales avec des groupes comme le Woodland Trust, des entreprises forestières, des agriculteurs et des conseils locaux.

**Impact** – Ce plan est important car il fixe des objectifs clairs et implique de nombreux acteurs différents. Augmenter la couverture forestière aidera l’environnement en améliorant la qualité de l’air, en soutenant la faune et en aidant à lutter contre le changement climatique. Il montre aussi que l’Irlande du Nord prend une mesure claire pour protéger et améliorer ses espaces naturels.

**Prochaine étape** – Au cours des cinq prochaines années, les actions du plan seront mises en œuvre. Cela inclut la plantation de beaucoup plus d’arbres et la sensibilisation du public à l’importance des arbres. Le gouvernement et ses partenaires suivront les progrès pour s’assurer que le rythme de plantation augmente suffisamment pour atteindre l’objectif de 2032.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Le nouveau plan de plantation d’arbres d’Irlande du Nord vise à tripler les plantations d’ici 2032 pour porter la couverture forestière de 8,6 % à 12 % d’ici 2050, marquant une avancée majeure pour l’environnement de la région.
articles.personality_title_es: Irlanda del Norte lanza su primer plan para triplicar la plantación de árboles para 2032
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Irlanda del Norte tiene actualmente solo un 8,6 % de su territorio cubierto de árboles, siendo una de las zonas menos boscosas de Europa. Otras partes del Reino Unido y de Irlanda tienen una cubierta forestal mucho mayor, como Escocia con un 19 % y la República de Irlanda con un 11 %.

**Qué pasó** – El gobierno de Irlanda del Norte ha creado su primer plan de acción para la plantación de árboles. Este plan busca más que triplicar la tasa actual de plantación de árboles para 2032. La meta es aumentar la cubierta forestal al 12 % para el año 2050. El plan incluye 10 acciones clave a realizar en los próximos cinco años. Fue desarrollado por el Departamento de Agricultura, Medio Ambiente y Asuntos Rurales junto con grupos como el Woodland Trust, empresas forestales, agricultores y ayuntamientos.

**Impacto** – Este plan es importante porque establece metas claras e involucra a muchos grupos diferentes trabajando juntos. Aumentar la cubierta de árboles ayudará al medio ambiente mejorando la calidad del aire, apoyando a la vida silvestre y ayudando a combatir el cambio climático. También muestra que Irlanda del Norte está tomando un paso claro para proteger y mejorar sus espacios naturales.

**Próximo paso** – Durante los próximos cinco años, se pondrán en práctica las acciones del plan. Esto incluye plantar muchos más árboles y aumentar la conciencia pública sobre la importancia de los árboles. El gobierno y sus socios seguirán el progreso para asegurarse de que la tasa de plantación crezca lo suficiente para cumplir la meta de 2032.

**Conclusión en una frase** – El nuevo plan de plantación de árboles de Irlanda del Norte busca triplicar la plantación para 2032 y aumentar la cubierta forestal del 8,6 % al 12 % para 2050, marcando un avance importante para el medio ambiente de la región.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vibrant Northern Irish landscape featuring young saplings being carefully planted in neat rows by silhouetted hands emerging from the earth, with a backdrop of rolling hills and a rising sun symbolizing growth and hope, using soft natural greens, browns, and gentle gold tones.

articles.title: The Path to Sharing Environmental Burdens Fairly
articles.summary:
In 1979, Hazel Johnson went door to door through her Chicago neighborhood with one question:...
The post The Path to Sharing Environmental Burdens Fairly appeared first on Earth911.
articles.content:
In 1979, Hazel Johnson went door to door through her Chicago neighborhood with one question: how many people in your household have cancer? Nearly every neighbor had an answer. Johnson’s public housing complex had been built on top of a former industrial waste dump. Moreover, more than 50 landfills, a chemical incinerator, steel mills, and abandoned toxic sites, were located within a few miles.

Hazel Johnson founded People for Community Recovery that year. By 1991, she was recognized as the Mother of the Environmental Justice Movement. She died in 2011, but the pattern she documented is still very much alive.

Across the United States, the people who live closest to landfills, waste transfer stations, and trash incinerators are, overwhelmingly, Black, Latino, and low-income. This is not an accident. Decades of research, government data, and community testimony point to the same conclusion: where the waste goes is a choice, and communities with less money and political power have repeatedly been left out of making it.

When everyone shares the burden of environmental damage, the politics of waste will change.

The Numbers Behind the Pattern

The data are hard to dismiss. More than half of all Americans living within about 2 miles of a hazardous waste facility are people of color. In 2022, half of all people living within one mile of a Superfund site (the most contaminated waste sites in the country, requiring federal cleanup) were people of color. Black Americans are 75% more likely than white Americans to live in what researchers call “fenceline communities,” meaning they live close enough to a polluting facility that they experience direct, ongoing exposure to its emissions and waste.

Trash incinerator siting shows the same pattern. Of the 68 remaining incinerators still operating in the United States, 15 of the 20 largest are located in majority Black and Latino communities. They are the oldest and dirtiest waste-burning programs. Their emissions include particulate matter, mercury, dioxins, and lead, which drive higher rates of asthma, respiratory disease, and heart problems in surrounding neighborhoods.

Illegal dumping makes the problem worse in the same communities. In one major American city, all ten neighborhoods with the highest rates of illegally dumped garbage were at least 60% Black or Latino. City wards where people of color make up the majority accounted for 79% of all illegally dumped waste. This is garbage generated elsewhere and transported to places where enforcement is weakest and political pressure is lowest.

Following the Path of Least Resistance

Researchers have a name for what happens in waste facility siting: “the path of least resistance.” A study by the University of Michigan and University of Montana tracked 30 years of data and found that industries seeking sites for hazardous waste operations consistently targeted minority and low-income communities because of politics. Wealthier, predominantly white communities organized and pushed back. “Not in my backyard” pressure worked for them. The facilities moved on to communities with less power to say no.

This unfair distribution of environmental health impacts plays out in specific, documented ways. When a scrapyard closed in a wealthy, predominantly white neighborhood on Chicago’s North Side, the same company got permits to open a new one on the city’s predominantly low-income, Latino Southeast Side. That area now hosts 250 EPA-regulated facilities and has become the only industrial corridor in Chicago zoned for hazardous-waste storage. Residents filed a civil rights complaint alleging the permitting process discriminated against them.

A 2025 study on landfill siting in South Carolina confirmed that Black and Hispanic residents were consistently more likely to live near landfills, and that lower-income households faced the same pattern. Of the two factors, race was the stronger predictor.

Three Communities, One Story

Cancer Alley, Louisiana

An 85-mile stretch of the Mississippi River between New Orleans and Baton Rouge is home to roughly 200 fossil fuel and petrochemical operations, reportedly the largest concentration anywhere in the Western Hemisphere. The people who live along this corridor are predominantly Black, and cancer rates in some communities along the river run up to 47 times higher than the level the EPA considers acceptable.

Sharon Lavigne has lived in St. James Parish her whole life. When a company proposed building a $1.25 billion plastics plant on land her ancestors once farmed, she founded the activist organization Rise St. James. After years of organizing, the plant was stopped. But as another resident told Human Rights Watch in 2024: “We’re dying from inhaling the industries’ pollution. I feel like it’s a death sentence.”

In June 2025, the Fifth Circuit Court of Appeals ruled that a lawsuit by Cancer Alley residents could proceed, finding that their claims reflected a “longstanding pattern and practice of racially discriminatory land-use decisions.”

The Ironbound, Newark, New Jersey

Newark’s Ironbound neighborhood is a dense, mostly Latino community boxed in by highways and the Passaic River. Its residents have been fighting the area’s large trash incinerator for nearly four decades.

Community activist Maria Lopez-Nuñez describes the neighborhood as a place “deemed ‘these lives don’t matter as much.'” In June 2025, a state commission approved a fourth gas-fired power plant in the neighborhood, adding to a pollution burden that residents say is already more than the community can bear.

Altgeld Gardens, Chicago, Illinois

In 1987, Hazel Johnson and 16 other protesters blockaded a new hazardous-waste landfill at the edge of Altgeld Gardens, turning away 57 trucks for more than 5 hours. All 17 were arrested.

The action stopped the landfill from opening and pushed the city toward a moratorium on new waste sites. Today, Johnson’s daughter, Cheryl, has taken over leadership of People for Community Recovery. In 2025, the Chicago Sun-Times found that federal rollbacks of EPA environmental justice programs are directly threatening the protections her mother spent her life fighting for.

The Health Costs Nobody Tallies

Living near waste facilities causes measurable harm. Studies link proximity to municipal landfills with low birth weight, bronchitis, and shortness of breath. Long-term exposure to hazardous waste sites is associated with cancer, neurological damage, and developmental delays in children.

These health burdens pile up. Waste facilities tend to push out health-promoting businesses, such as clinics, grocery stores, and parks. A 2025 analysis in Environmental Research found that the negative effects of living near waste infrastructure fell most heavily on lower-income communities of color, with no corresponding economic benefit flowing back to those communities.

Robert Taylor, founder of Concerned Citizens of St. John in Cancer Alley, has described the unbearable human cost of environmental injustice: “Seeing my family and community, especially the young kids, get sick and die is so painful, especially because this is all preventable.”

What’s Holding Back Change

Environmental justice advocates are careful to separate symptoms from causes. Individual facility decisions are symptoms, they argue, but the causes go deeper: zoning laws concentrate industrial uses in low-income areas, permitting processes allow public comments without giving communities real political power, and there has been a steady rollback of federal environmental justice enforcement since early 2025.

One key federal protection has already been weakened. Title VI of the Civil Rights Act prohibits programs that receive federal funding from discriminating on the basis of race. But in August 2024, a federal judge in Louisiana issued a permanent injunction barring the EPA from enforcing its disparate-impact rules under Title VI, which would have allowed communities to challenge a new facility on the grounds that it added to a discriminatory pattern of siting decisions.

In November 2024, the EPA’s independent advisory council recommended requiring cumulative impact assessments before new waste facilities are granted a site approvals, meaning regulators would have to account for all existing pollution in a community before adding more. That recommendation has not been adopted.

The EPA’s EJScreen mapping tool, updated in July 2024 but now hidden after recent EPA decisions, lets anyone enter a ZIP Code to see how much pollution burden their community already carries. The data made the pattern visible, but now the Trump Administration wants America to look away.

What You Can Do

The biggest fixes require policy changes, stronger enforcement, and political will. But individuals have real roles to play, from cutting the total waste that needs to go somewhere to supporting the communities on the front lines.

As an individual or household:

Reduce waste at the source. Every pound diverted by buying less, repairing more, or choosing durable goods is one less pound that ends up in a landfill or incinerator somewhere. Other articles in this series cover the highest-impact categories: food, flexible plastic packaging, textiles, and electronics.

Demand the EPA restore access to the EJScreen tool. It shows environmental burden by location. Understanding the pattern in your area is the first step toward acting on it.

Recycle accurately. When household recycling bins are contaminated with the wrong materials, recyclable material often ends up in landfills and incinerators. Use the Earth911 Recycling Search to find verified drop-off locations for hard-to-recycle items.

At the community and policy level:

Support extended producer responsibility (EPR) laws in your state. These laws shift waste management costs back to manufacturers, reducing the volume of low-value material flowing into community-level infrastructure. Oregon, Colorado, California, Maine, Minnesota, Maryland, and Washington have active packaging EPR programs.

Push for cumulative impact standards in permitting decisions. New waste facilities should not be approved without accounting for all the pollution a community already carries.

Support frontline organizations directly. People for Community Recovery, Rise St. James, Concerned Citizens of St. John, and similar groups do the sustained, ground-level work that makes policy change possible. They need resources.

Attend public comment periods for waste facility permits in your region. Regulatory agencies are legally required to hold these hearings. Showing up is one of the few moments where community voices have a formal role in the process.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the ongoing environmental justice movement addressing the disproportionate burden of waste facilities on marginalized communities, documenting significant health and social impacts while outlining actionable steps and policy recommendations to achieve fairer environmental outcomes. It focuses on a meaningful, broad societal issue with detailed context and examples, inspiring hope for change through community activism and policy reform.
articles.personality_title: Communities Fight for Fair Share of Environmental Burdens
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many low-income and minority neighborhoods in the United States live near dangerous waste sites like landfills and incinerators. This has been a problem for decades, starting with Hazel Johnson’s work in Chicago in 1979, who found many neighbors suffering from cancer because of pollution from nearby industrial waste.

**What happened** – Hazel Johnson started a group called People for Community Recovery to fight these issues. Today, research shows that Black, Latino, and poor communities are still more likely to live near harmful waste facilities. These locations cause health problems like asthma and cancer. Activists in places like Louisiana’s Cancer Alley, Newark’s Ironbound, and Chicago’s Altgeld Gardens continue to push back against new polluting facilities.

**Impact** – This situation is important because it shows how pollution and waste are not shared equally. Communities with less money and power face more pollution and health risks. Studies prove this is not random but a result of unfair decisions, where polluting facilities are placed where people are less able to fight back. This hurts people’s health and quality of life without giving them any benefit.

**What’s next step** – Advocates want stronger laws to stop these unfair patterns, including better rules that consider all pollution in an area before approving new waste sites. They also want tools like the EPA’s EJScreen map to be available so people can see pollution levels in their community. Individuals can help by reducing waste, recycling correctly, and supporting local groups working for change.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Communities and activists are working to make sure environmental burdens like waste facilities are shared more fairly, protecting health and improving justice.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les communautés luttent pour un partage équitable des charges environnementales
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – De nombreux quartiers pauvres et minoritaires aux États-Unis vivent près de sites dangereux comme des décharges et des incinérateurs. Ce problème existe depuis des décennies, comme l’a montré Hazel Johnson à Chicago en 1979, qui a découvert que beaucoup de ses voisins souffraient de cancer à cause de la pollution des déchets industriels proches.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Hazel Johnson a créé un groupe nommé People for Community Recovery pour lutter contre ces problèmes. Aujourd’hui, des recherches montrent que les communautés noires, latino-américaines et pauvres vivent toujours plus près des sites de déchets dangereux. Ces lieux causent des problèmes de santé comme l’asthme et le cancer. Des militants en Louisiane, dans le quartier Ironbound à Newark, et à Altgeld Gardens à Chicago continuent de s’opposer à ces installations polluantes.

**Impact** – Cette situation est importante car elle montre que la pollution et les déchets ne sont pas partagés de manière égale. Les communautés avec moins d’argent et de pouvoir subissent plus de pollution et de risques pour la santé. Les études prouvent que ce n’est pas un hasard, mais le résultat de décisions injustes qui placent les installations polluantes là où les gens ont moins de moyens pour s’y opposer. Cela nuit à leur santé et à leur qualité de vie sans leur apporter de bénéfices.

**Prochaine étape** – Les défenseurs veulent des lois plus strictes pour arrêter ces inégalités, notamment des règles qui prennent en compte toute la pollution dans une zone avant d’autoriser de nouveaux sites de déchets. Ils souhaitent aussi que des outils comme la carte EJScreen de l’EPA soient accessibles pour que chacun puisse voir le niveau de pollution dans sa communauté. Les individus peuvent aider en réduisant leurs déchets, en recyclant correctement et en soutenant les groupes locaux qui travaillent pour le changement.

**En une phrase** – Les communautés et les militants travaillent pour que les charges environnementales, comme les sites de déchets, soient partagées plus équitablement, protégeant la santé et améliorant la justice.
articles.personality_title_es: Las comunidades luchan por una distribución justa de las cargas ambientales
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchos vecindarios pobres y de minorías en Estados Unidos viven cerca de sitios peligrosos como vertederos e incineradoras. Este problema existe desde hace décadas, como lo mostró Hazel Johnson en Chicago en 1979, cuando descubrió que muchos vecinos tenían cáncer debido a la contaminación de los desechos industriales cercanos.

**Qué pasó** – Hazel Johnson fundó un grupo llamado People for Community Recovery para combatir estos problemas. Hoy, las investigaciones demuestran que las comunidades negras, latinas y de bajos ingresos siguen viviendo más cerca de sitios de desechos peligrosos. Estos lugares causan problemas de salud como asma y cáncer. Activistas en lugares como Cancer Alley en Luisiana, Ironbound en Newark y Altgeld Gardens en Chicago siguen luchando contra estas instalaciones contaminantes.

**Impacto** – Esta situación es importante porque muestra que la contaminación y los desechos no se distribuyen de manera justa. Las comunidades con menos dinero y poder sufren más contaminación y riesgos para la salud. Los estudios prueban que no es casualidad, sino el resultado de decisiones injustas que colocan las instalaciones contaminantes donde las personas tienen menos capacidad para oponer resistencia. Esto afecta su salud y calidad de vida sin ofrecerles beneficios.

**Próximo paso** – Los defensores quieren leyes más fuertes para detener estos patrones injustos, incluyendo reglas que consideren toda la contaminación de una zona antes de aprobar nuevos sitios de desechos. También quieren que herramientas como el mapa EJScreen de la EPA estén disponibles para que las personas conozcan la contaminación en su comunidad. Los individuos pueden ayudar reduciendo sus residuos, reciclando bien y apoyando a los grupos locales que trabajan por el cambio.

**Una frase clave** – Comunidades y activistas trabajan para que las cargas ambientales, como los sitios de desechos, se compartan de forma más justa, protegiendo la salud y mejorando la justicia.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of three intertwined community gardens shaped like neighborhoods, each bordered by symbolic barriers made of faded industrial pipes and smokestacks, with vibrant roots and vines growing through the cracks, representing resilience and unity overcoming pollution and environmental injustice.

articles.title: Climate-smart apartments designed to beat the heat
articles.summary: While most of France suffered from last week's heatwave, a certain number of Montpellier residents were in total comfort in their apartments – without air conditioning.  From Middle Eastern-inspired latticework maximising ventilation to curved facades acting as giant sunshades, everything has been designed to keep the heat at bay.  And it works: indoor temperatures rarely rise above 25°C. In this edition, we look at the design keeping people cool in the heat.
articles.content:
Climate-smart apartments designed to beat the heat

Environment

To display this content from YouTube, you must enable advertisement tracking and audience measurement. Accept Manage my choices One of your browser extensions seems to be blocking the video player from loading. To watch this content, you may need to disable it on this site. Try again Cover image: down to earth © France2 02:29

02:29 min Share

From the show

Reading time 1 min

While most of France suffered from last week's heatwave, a certain number of Montpellier residents were in total comfort in their apartments – without air conditioning. From Middle Eastern-inspired latticework maximising ventilation to curved facades acting as giant sunshades, everything has been designed to keep the heat at bay. And it works: indoor temperatures rarely rise above 25°C. In this edition, we look at the design keeping people cool in the heat.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on climate-smart apartment designs in Montpellier that effectively keep indoor temperatures comfortable without air conditioning during heatwaves. This represents a meaningful environmental innovation with broad significance for public comfort and energy efficiency amid climate change.
articles.personality_title: Montpellier apartments stay cool without air conditioning during heatwave
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Last week, much of France experienced a severe heatwave, making it very hot for many people. Usually, air conditioning is used to keep indoor spaces cool during such times.

**What happened** – In Montpellier, some new apartments were built with special designs inspired by Middle Eastern architecture. These include latticework that allows air to flow through and curved walls that act like big sunshades. Because of these features, the apartments stay cool without needing air conditioning. Inside, temperatures rarely go above 25°C.

**Impact** – This design shows a way to keep homes comfortable during heatwaves while saving energy. It helps people avoid using air conditioning, which can use a lot of electricity and add to climate change. It also shows how building design can adapt to hotter weather in a simple and natural way.

**What's next step** – These ideas could be used in other buildings in France and around the world. Architects and builders might study this design to create more homes that stay cool naturally. This could help many people live better during hot summers.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Specially designed apartments in Montpellier stay cool during heatwaves without air conditioning by using natural ventilation and sunshades.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des appartements à Montpellier restent frais sans climatisation lors de la canicule
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La semaine dernière, une forte canicule a touché une grande partie de la France, rendant les températures très élevées pour beaucoup de personnes. Habituellement, on utilise la climatisation pour garder les intérieurs frais.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – À Montpellier, de nouveaux appartements ont été construits avec des designs spéciaux inspirés de l’architecture du Moyen-Orient. Ils possèdent des treillis qui laissent passer l’air et des façades courbes qui font office de grands pare-soleil. Grâce à ces caractéristiques, les appartements restent frais sans climatisation. À l’intérieur, la température dépasse rarement 25°C.

**Impact** – Ce design montre une façon de garder les logements confortables pendant les canicules tout en économisant de l’énergie. Cela évite l’utilisation de la climatisation, qui consomme beaucoup d’électricité et contribue au changement climatique. Cela prouve aussi que l’architecture peut s’adapter naturellement à des étés plus chauds.

**Prochaine étape** – Ces idées pourraient être appliquées dans d’autres bâtiments en France et dans le monde. Les architectes et constructeurs pourraient s’inspirer de ce design pour créer plus de logements qui restent frais naturellement. Cela pourrait améliorer la vie de nombreuses personnes lors des étés chauds.

**Une phrase clé** – Des appartements spécialement conçus à Montpellier restent frais pendant la canicule sans climatisation grâce à la ventilation naturelle et aux pare-soleil.
articles.personality_title_es: Apartamentos en Montpellier se mantienen frescos sin aire acondicionado durante ola de calor
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La semana pasada, gran parte de Francia sufrió una ola de calor intensa, haciendo que las temperaturas fueran muy altas para muchas personas. Normalmente, se usa aire acondicionado para mantener frescos los interiores.

**Qué pasó** – En Montpellier, se construyeron nuevos apartamentos con diseños especiales inspirados en la arquitectura del Medio Oriente. Estos incluyen celosías que permiten que el aire circule y fachadas curvas que funcionan como grandes sombrillas. Gracias a estas características, los apartamentos se mantienen frescos sin aire acondicionado. En el interior, la temperatura rara vez supera los 25°C.

**Impacto** – Este diseño muestra una manera de mantener las casas cómodas durante las olas de calor mientras se ahorra energía. Ayuda a las personas a evitar usar aire acondicionado, que consume mucha electricidad y contribuye al cambio climático. También muestra cómo el diseño de edificios puede adaptarse al clima más cálido de forma simple y natural.

**Próximo paso** – Estas ideas podrían usarse en otros edificios en Francia y en todo el mundo. Arquitectos y constructores podrían estudiar este diseño para crear más hogares que se mantengan frescos de forma natural. Esto podría ayudar a muchas personas a vivir mejor durante los veranos calurosos.

**Una frase clave** – Apartamentos especialmente diseñados en Montpellier se mantienen frescos durante las olas de calor sin aire acondicionado usando ventilación natural y sombrillas solares.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a softly curved apartment building with intricate Middle Eastern-style latticework on the windows, gently filtering sunlight and casting delicate shadows, surrounded by a calm, sunlit urban setting in natural, earthy tones.
